schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch correct the LXX/Vulgate joining of 'Judah' to the preceding words and reject Bertheau's notion that 'Israel' means only the northern tribes — it is one national chronicle.
Clarke, following Jarchi, hears Ezra the compiler citing a book carried to Babylon; Wesley takes 'the book' for the public records of the kingdom.
Gill reads the opening clause as a note that an exact account of all Israel had been kept, from which the writer drew.
Henry makes the carrying-away a standing warning: say of every calamity 'it was for my transgression,' that God may be justified when he judges.

themes: one nation of Israel and Judah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the introduction to the whole chapter (answering v.34) and fix its relation to Nehemiah 11: this is a register of the former inhabitants of Jerusalem only.
Clarke lays out the fourfold order since Joshua — priests, Levites, Israelites, Nethinim (Gibeonite temple-slaves); Wesley stresses the reunited people under the general name 'Israelites.'
Gill takes the first inhabitants for those who returned first on Cyrus's proclamation and reoccupied their former cities.

themes: the four classes of the people

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the sentence is verbatim Nehemiah 11:4a, but the added clause on Ephraim and Manasseh, absent from Nehemiah, shows the compiler's ordinary freedom, not invention.
Gill: Judah and Benjamin, being the largest number carried away and returned, furnished the bulk of Jerusalem's resettlers.
Clarke widens it to include men of Ephraim and Manasseh, needed to populate so large a city.

Keil and Ellicott confront the divergence from Nehemiah 11:4: Uthai is Athaiah and Imri a contraction of Amariah, but the intervening ancestors differ because each writer selected different names from a longer stem, not because the clans differ.
Gill traces Uthai through Ammihud, Omri, Imri, and Bani to Pharez, noting he is the Athaiah of Nehemiah with differently reckoned forebears.
Clarke reads the list as the earliest returners with Zerubbabel; Wesley ascribes the diversity of names to the Hebrew custom of multiple names and the change of times.

themes: Uthai of the line of Pharez

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott recover the true reading 'the Shelanite' (from Shelah, Judah's son, Num. 26:20) behind the mispointed 'Shilonite,' supported by the LXX, the Targum, and Nehemiah 11:5, where Asaiah answers to Maaseiah.
Gill warns these are not men of Ephraimite Shiloh but the Shelanites of Judah, and cites the Targum's 'tribe of Shelah.'

Keil fixes the plural 'their brethren' as reaching over the three Judahite heads, and Ellicott notes the Chronicler counts the Zarhites (690) where Nehemiah drops them and gives only a Parzite total — each pruning the common source.
Gill notes only that Zerah was another son of Judah.

themes: Jeuel a clan not a man

schools.summary: Keil lays out the four Benjamite heads and the total 956; Ellicott argues the divergences from Nehemiah 11:7 are textual — corrupt spellings and dropped conjunctions obscuring an underlying agreement.
Gill takes these for Benjamites who went into and returned from captivity, or dwelt in Jerusalem before.

Ellicott reconstructs behind the two rolls a single register of five Benjamite houses — Sallu, Hodaviah, Ibneiah, Joel, Meshullam — their names contracted and miscopied into apparent difference; Keil gives the pedigrees and the total 956.
Gill notes only that Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam, with their ancestors, were Benjamites met nowhere else.
Gill records the Benjamite total of 956 as greatly exceeding the reckoning of Judah put together.

Wesley reconciles the total (956 here, 928 in Nehemiah 11:8) by supposing Nehemiah counts only the lot-chosen, to whom freewill settlers were added, or that some first placed there had since died or removed — the rolls taken at different moments.

Henry honours these chief fathers as Israelites indeed, whose spirit God stirred up to seek Zion when many stayed behind in Babylon, and hears the ingathering of Judah and Israel foretold in Hosea 1:11 and Ezekiel 37:22.

themes: the ingathering of Judah and Israel

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jachin not as men but as three of the twenty-four priestly courses of 1 Chron. 24 (Jehoiarib first, Jedaiah second, Jachin twenty-first); Nehemiah's 'Jehoiarib son of Jedaiah' is a scribal slip binding two course-names into a descent.
Gill takes them simply for priests who returned and dwelt at Jerusalem.
Henry: it was the priests' praise to come up with the first, for who but the Lord's ministers should lead in a good work and keep knowledge?

Keil identifies Azariah with the Azariah of 1 Chron. 6:13, prince of the house of God; Ellicott weighs a Meraioth/Ahitub transposition and the Seraiah/Azariah variant of Nehemiah 11:11, allowing the name may be official, a priestly house rather than a man.
Clarke and Wesley make him not the high priest (Jeshua) but the ruler or sagan, the high priest's deputy over the temple guard — the 'second priest' of 2 Kings 25:18.
Gill traces his descent through Meshullam (Shallum), Zadok, and Meraioth to Ahitub, the high-priestly house.
Henry: the sagan, deputy to Joshua, perhaps more diligent than the high priest; rulers are needed in God's house to see his laws observed by priests and people alike.

themes: the ruler of the house of God
an official title or a man

schools.summary: Ellicott shows Adaiah's line omits three names Nehemiah keeps, and that Maasiai/Amashsai and its variant spellings mask one identical descent — proof that three whole courses, two clans, and the temple-ruler dwelt in Jerusalem; Keil sets the two heads in place.
Gill notes the three names (Pelaliah, Amzi, Zechariah) omitted between Jeroham and Pashur.
Clarke marvels at the prodigious 1,760 ecclesiastics for one temple; Wesley explains 'mighty men of valour' as the courage needed to execute the censures of the law and guard the sacred things.
Ellicott doubts so many priestly clans dwelt in the city, reads the number as summing the whole section, notes Nehemiah's total of 1,192 and that 'for' is wanting in the Hebrew.
Gill notes the priestly total exceeded Judah and Benjamin together.
Henry: able ministers of the New Testament, mighty men of valour fit for a work that after Babylon demanded courage and vigour.

Henry reads the whole Levitical section as religion restored to order: the tabernacle before the temple, the Davidic model revived, every one knowing his charge, the temple a figure of the heavenly courts where they rest not day nor night.
Gill names the first Levite, Shemaiah, of the sons of Merari.
Ellicott notes the verse is verbatim the same as Nehemiah 11:15 save the Merarite deduction and one extra ancestor there.

Keil sorts the descents — Mattaniah from Asaph the Gershonite, Obadiah through Galal from Jeduthun the Merarite, Berechiah dwelling in the Netophathite villages — naming the heads of the several musical houses.
Gill traces Mattaniah through Micah and Zichri to Asaph the singer of David's day, and thinks Bakbakkar the same as the Bakbukiah of Nehemiah 11:17.

themes: the guilds of Asaph and Jeduthun
the song of the restored house

schools.summary: Keil traces Obadiah to Jeduthun and sets Berechiah in the villages of the Netophathites near Bethlehem, where a company of Levites had their homes.
Gill identifies Obadiah son of Shemaiah with the Abda son of Shammua of the parallel.
Henry: some Levites dwelt in the villages, perhaps for want of room in the city, yet no less employed in the service, coming up in their courses.

themes: a ministry rooted in ordinary life

schools.summary: Ellicott makes the porters' names hereditary guilds, the Levitical offices descending father to son like Roman civil posts and ascribed to David; Shallum is Meshelemiah, chief at the honoured eastern king's gate.
Gill names them the porters, keepers of the gates of the tabernacle.
Henry honours the office: better to keep the door of God's house than to dwell in the tents of wickedness.

Keil reads 'until now in the king's gate' as evidence of a pre-exilic origin for the register; Ellicott hears in 'the camps of the sons of Levi' archaic Mosaic terminology tying the temple wardership back to the wilderness tent (Num. 3:23).
Clarke and Wesley explain the king's gate as the east gate by which the kings of Judah went up to the temple, under which all the rest are comprehended, kept by courses.
Gill: the porters waited at the east gate, placed after the captivity in the same order as before.

themes: wardership rooted in the Mosaic sanctuary

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott set Shallum the Korahite of the house of Ebiasaph as keeper of the thresholds of the tent (=temple), and read 'the host of the LORD' as the encampment of Jehovah, the tabernacle whose single entrance his fathers guarded (cf. 2 Chron. 31:2).

Clarke (with Jarchi) makes the keepers of the entry those who admitted none but priests during the service; Wesley: all things now restored to their primitive wilderness order, the sons taking up their fathers' offices.
Gill traces Shallum up to Ebiasaph the son of Korah.

Keil and Ellicott make Phinehas prince over the keepers during his father Eleazar's high-priesthood (Joshua's day), and read 'the LORD was with him' as a benediction 'Jahve be with him' — Ellicott finding in it a hint of belief in life after death.
Gill takes the ruler for Phinehas the priest of Moses' day and reads the closing clause as a statement of fact that God was with him.
Clarke, from the Targum, keeps the historical sense: Phinehas ruled from the day the tabernacle was set up, and the Word of the Lord was his assistant.

debates.question: Is 'and the LORD was with him' a statement of fact or a benediction?

sides.position: a blessing, 'Jahve be with him,' spoken over a departed worthy
a historical statement that God was indeed with Phinehas

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read the verse as an abrupt return to the Davidic age: Zechariah, firstborn of Meshelemiah, held the north gate under David (1 Chron. 26:2, 14), though Keil owns he cannot say why he alone is named.
Gill refers it rather to one after the captivity who kept the order established under David.
Wesley makes him a chief porter of the door into the tabernacle, and bids those without a temple be thankful for a tabernacle.

themes: the Davidic ordering of the gates

schools.summary: Ellicott ranges the 212 warders against Nehemiah's 172, Ezra's 139, and David's 93, and both he and Keil feel the difficulty of the clause that David and Samuel ordained them, since Samuel died before David's accession — true, Ellicott says, in spirit if not in the letter.
Wesley resolves it by division of labour: Samuel, seeing the Levites increased, began to order their ministration, and David revived and perfected the design.
Gill takes the 212 as the number fixed in David's days, which might not be fewer.

debates.question: How could both David and Samuel have ordained the gatekeepers?

sides.position: Samuel began the order and David revived and perfected it
true in spirit or by tradition, but not literally, since Samuel died before David reigned

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read 'house of the tabernacle' as added so 'house of the LORD' is not confined to Solomon's temple, since none was built in David's day, the watch disposed to the four winds.
Gill: the charge was hereditary, the posterity of David's appointees succeeding them at the gates.
Wesley distinguishes the Mosaic tabernacle at Gibeon, meant here, from David's tent for the ark, and glosses 'by wards' as by turns or courses.

Ellicott renders 'to the four winds the warders stood,' on the four sides of the tent and later of the temple enclosure; Keil sets the fourfold watch within the weekly sabbath rotation of the courses.
Gill notes the four quarters and the Targum's four-and-twenty wards.
Ellicott: the warder-families, like the singers, lived on farms in the villages round Jerusalem and came up in weekly rotation (Neh. 12:29), 'after seven days' meaning every sabbath.
Wesley describes the relief: the courses changed each seventh day, the new-comers tarrying till the next sabbath, serving beside the resident chiefs.
Gill: the brethren dwelt in the villages assigned them.
Henry: each had his week's attendance in his turn.

themes: a ministry rooted in the villages

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'in fixed trust were they, the four heroes of the warders,' set over the chambers and treasuries, and would move the paragraph so that vv.28-34 review the other Levitical charges; Keil names the chambers and treasuries their charge.
Gill: the four chief porters were never changed nor went to the villages, always superintending the rest.
Wesley: constantly on the spot, set over the treasuries where the sacred utensils were kept.

themes: the heart of a rotating watch

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: they passed the night about the house because the ward was theirs, and the opening of the door — the key (maphteach, cf. Judg. 3:25, Isa. 22:22) — pertained to them every morning.
Gill: they lodged in chambers on the outward wall, near to do their office.
Henry: it was no mean thing to keep the charge of the house of God.

Keil takes these for the costly vessels brought from the treasure-chamber for the service; Ellicott stresses 'by tale' — the gold and silver counted in and out to make sure none was purloined (cf. Ezra 8:20).
Gill: the Levites brought the vessels the priests used in sacrificing and returned them to their places.
Henry: entrusted with the plate by tale — every one knowing his charge in the house of the God of order.

themes: counted in and out by tale

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott set others over the ordinary vessels of daily service and the stores of fine flour, wine, oil, frankincense, and spices for the offerings and holy perfumes (Exod. 25:6).
Gill: the instruments of the sanctuary committed to their care, delivered out and seen returned.
Henry: every one knew his charge, for that which is everybody's work will be nobody's.
Clarke: only the priests might make the ointment, all others forbidden on pain of death (Exod. 30:34-38); Wesley: the Levites kept it but could not make it.
Keil and Ellicott identify the holy anointing oil of Exod. 30:23-29, a parenthetic note that its compounding was the priest's duty alone.
Gill: though the Levites had care of the spices, they might not make the ointment, only the priests.

themes: Levite and priest each within his bound
the fenced holiness of the sanctuary

schools.summary: Keil sets Mattithiah the Korahite 'on good faith' over the panbakings for the meat-offerings; Ellicott reads 'things made in the pans' as cakes (habittim, cf. Ezek. 4:3), the duty hereditary in Shallum's branch.
Gill identifies Mattithiah as the firstborn of Shallum the Korahite.
Wesley: he was to see that fine flour was provided for the cakes made in the pans.

themes: the Korahite clan of the gates

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott commit the shewbread to the Kohathite Korahites, prepared fresh for the priests to lay on the golden table every sabbath (Lev. 24:5-9), the 'bread of the pile' or 'of the presence.'
Gill: twelve loaves set weekly before the ark, the old removed; under the second temple the family of Garmu kept this work.

Keil and Ellicott read the subscription to vv.14-32: the singers, heads of Levitical houses, dwelling in the temple cells 'free' — immune from every other ministry — under obligation to a perpetually recurring, though not literally unceasing, service (cf. Rev. 4:8).
Gill: the singers, chief of the fathers, the masters of the song who presided in that work.
Wesley: freed from all other employment to attend the sacred song day and night, especially at the morning and evening service, that God might be continually praised.

Ellicott reads the subscription closing the Levites, the names standing for clan-chiefs; Keil marks how it clears the way for Saul's genealogy, repeated to connect the kingship with the lists and introduce 1 Chron. 10.
Gill: the chief fathers of the Levites, chief throughout their generations.
Wesley finds a providence that the Levites were obliged to settle at Jerusalem, that they might constantly attend God's service.

Wesley says the genealogy is repeated to make way for the following history; Clarke presses the textual crux of Maachah, 'wife' or 'sister' — a single letter's difference — judging 'wife' the true reading against the Hebrew's 'sister.'

Henry, admitting transcribers' errors in these books, suspects the repetition arose from a copyist's eye slipping from one 'these dwelt in Jerusalem' to another, yet lets it stand: 'redundancies do no harm.'
Gill notes Jehiel the father of Gibeon is named here though absent from the parallel in 1 Chron. 8:29.

debates.question: Is the repeated genealogy of Saul deliberate or a scribal accident?

sides.position: a designed bridge from the registers to the history of Saul
possibly an accidental duplication a copyist let stand

schools.summary: Keil lists the sons of Jehiel; Ellicott reads the names theologically — Zur, 'Rock,' a divine title, with a discussion of Shaddai as 'giver of rain' — and conjectures that Baal and Nadab may be one name, 'Baal-nadab,' which would leave Ner out of place.

outliers.note: Conjectures that 'Baal' and 'Nadab' should be read as a single compound name, Baal-nadab ('Baal is prince'), in which case 'Ner' would be misplaced in the list.

themes: names of the Rock and the Almighty

schools.summary: Keil carries the line to the first king — Ner begat Kish, Kish begat Saul, Saul begat Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Eshbaal — the register reaching its purpose of introducing the narrative of Saul's death in 1 Chron. 10.
Henry reads the repetition as the hinge between the genealogies and the history, passing the reader from the tribe to its king, and lets the redundant copy stand: redundancies do no harm.
Clarke takes it simply as the repetition of 1 Chron. 8:33, the descent set down again to serve the coming narrative.
Keil carries the surviving line through Jonathan's son Meribbaal and Micah to the sons Pithon, Melech, Tahrea, and Ahaz — the house of Saul preserved through the lame son David spared.

Clarke notes 'Ahaz' is supplied from 1 Chron. 8:35 and printed in italics, warning that such editorial additions, left unmarked, may in time harden into the received text; and that these verses are a repetition of 1 Chron. 8:29-38.

Keil carries the line Moza, Binea, Rephaiah, Eleasah, Azel in the tightening formula 'his son, his son'; Ellicott notes Rephaiah stands as the contracted 'Rapha' in the parallel, a token of the independent, better-preserved source.

Keil names Azel's six sons, the last leaves of Saul's tree; Ellicott argues the omission of the sons of Eshek and Ulam (found in 1 Chron. 8:38-40) is not inadvertence but the design of the Chronicler or of his source, as in the abridgment of 1 Chron. 6.
Henry wonders whether the whole repetition owed to a copyist's slip, yet is content to let it stand: redundancies do no harm.
Clarke simply names it a repetition of the earlier passage.

debates.question: Is the closing repetition and abridgment by design or partly accident?

sides.position: deliberate — the omission and repetition are the writer's own design
possibly an accidental duplication the writer let stand

themes: the omission of Eshek and Ulam
